text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Hannah Price
Elm Grove Medical Centre

14 Elm Grove, Southwark, London, SE5 8QT
Dear Dr Hannah Price
Michael Turner, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: M839204 NHS No. 7483920174
Flat 3, 22 Willow Close, London, SE15 3HT 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He is known to have focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset from prior EEG findings, and works outdoors in gardening and grounds maintenance. I last assessed him on 16 May 2025. Since then he reports that his more significant episodes have occurred almost exclusively after nights of curtailed rest rather than on fully rested days, particularly following early starts during warm spells. He notes that heat and prolonged exertion vary with the seasons and tend to amplify his usual warning symptoms when he has not had adequate rest.

His current antiseizure therapy is Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. He remains adherent without missed doses. He describes stereotyped focal aware seizures comprising a rising epigastric sensation with brief déjà vu, lasting 30–60 seconds, with preserved awareness. Over the past three months he has had approximately five focal aware events, four of which followed nights with reduced rest and consecutive long outdoor shifts during the recent warm period. He has had one focal impaired-awareness episode in early August, again the morning after reduced rest, with brief behavioural arrest and lip smacking, lasting around two minutes, followed by 20–30 minutes of postictal fatigue. There have been no generalised tonic–clonic seizures.

He denies new neurological symptoms. Mood is stable; he does not report irritability on Levetiracetam. No rash, diplopia or ataxia on Lamotrigine. He occasionally experiences light-headedness after prolonged work in high temperatures, which he attributes to inadequate hydration; this has not been associated with loss of awareness. Examination today was unremarkable with normal speech, cranial nerves and coordination.

Given the seasonal pattern of outdoor exertion and his report that clinically significant events happen chiefly on days following curtailed rest, we discussed practical measures he is already taking at work, including structured breaks, hydration, and planning heavier tasks earlier in the day when feasible. From a seizure control perspective he prefers not to escalate medication at present, which is reasonable in view of the low frequency and absence of generalised convulsions. I have provided a prescription to continue Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. unchanged.

I have arranged an updated ambulatory EEG to reassess interictal activity, as the last study was over two years ago, and will repeat serum levels for Lamotrigine and a general metabolic screen (U&E, LFT, FBC) given increased exertion during warm weather. We also reviewed safety at work: he does not climb ladders or operate heavy machinery alone, and he works with a colleague aware of his focal warning symptoms. He confirms he is not driving.

I will see him again in four months or sooner if he experiences focal impaired-awareness events that are not preceded by curtailed rest, or any generalised seizures. He will contact the clinic if frequency increases.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the past three months he has had approximately five focal aware events

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date 02 Oct 2025. Letter states: over the past three months he had approximately five focal aware seizures, and one focal impaired-awareness seizure in early August (within the same 3‑month period). No GTCs. Per rule 7, when multiple frequencies exist, select the highest frequency. The highest explicit frequency is five events over three months. Therefore, label as 5 per 3 month. Quote taken from the explicit statement of frequency.
seizure_frequency_number: Over the past three months he has had approximately five focal aware events

flaws.description: Gold (5 per 3 month) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
